description: This figure presents a line chart illustrating the dynamic recrystallization (DRX) average grain size of a low-density high-strength Fe-Mn-Al-C steel as a function of deformation temperature (900–1150 °C) at four constant strain rates: 0.01, 0.1, 1, and 10 s⁻¹. The four curves, distinguished by black squares, red circles, blue triangles, and magenta stars in the legend, respectively, demonstrate that the DRX grain size increases monotonically with decreasing strain rate and increasing temperature. At the most severe condition of 1150 °C and 0.01 s⁻¹, the grain size exceeds 70 μm, indicating rapid grain growth and a coarse-grained structure. Conversely, at lower temperatures (900–950 °C) and higher strain rates (1–10 s⁻¹), the grains remain small (approximately 4–12 μm), but DRX is insufficient, leaving remnant original large grains and yielding poor microstructural homogeneity. These data support the development of a grain size prediction model and identify optimal hot working windows—specifically 950–1000 °C at 0.01–0.1 s⁻¹ and 1050–1100 °C at 1–10 s⁻¹—for achieving fine, uniform, fully recrystallized microstructures.
